 {"id":"ntm-5b7","title":"Unit Tests: internal/tutorial, updater, watcher (0% → 80%)","description":"# Unit Tests: Remaining Packages

## Packages Covered
- internal/tutorial (0%)
- internal/updater (0%)
- internal/watcher (0%)

---

## internal/tutorial (tutorial.go)

### Model Tests
- [ ] TestNewTutorialModel - initial state
- [ ] TestTutorialSlideCount - 9 slides
- [ ] TestTutorialNavigation_Next - right/space/enter
- [ ] TestTutorialNavigation_Prev - left
- [ ] TestTutorialNavigation_Jump - 1-9 keys

### Animation Tests
- [ ] TestTutorialTransitions - all transition types
- [ ] TestTutorialTypingAnimation - text reveal
- [ ] TestTutorialSkipAnimation - 's' key skips

### Slide Content
- [ ] TestSlideContent_Welcome - first slide
- [ ] TestSlideContent_AllSlides - all slides have content

---

## internal/updater (updater.go)

### Version Comparison
- [ ] TestCompareVersions_Newer - newer version detected
- [ ] TestCompareVersions_Same - same version
- [ ] TestCompareVersions_Older - older version
- [ ] TestCompareVersions_Prerelease - alpha < release
- [ ] TestCompareVersions_VPrefix - handles v prefix
- [ ] TestCompareVersions_Invalid - invalid versions

### Update Check
- [ ] TestCheckForUpdate - API call succeeds
- [ ] TestCheckForUpdate_NoNetwork - handles network error
- [ ] TestCheckForUpdate_RateLimit - handles rate limit
- [ ] TestCheckForUpdate_Timeout - respects timeout

### Version Parsing
- [ ] TestParseVersion - major.minor.patch
- [ ] TestParseVersion_Prerelease - with -alpha suffix
- [ ] TestParseVersion_Invalid - returns error

---

## internal/watcher (debouncer.go)

### Debouncer
- [ ] TestNewDebouncer - creates with duration
- [ ] TestDebouncerTrigger_Single - single trigger fires
- [ ] TestDebouncerTrigger_Multiple - multiple triggers debounced
- [ ] TestDebouncerTiming - fires after delay
- [ ] TestDebouncerConcurrent - thread-safe

### File Watcher (if implemented)
- [ ] TestWatchFile - detects file change
- [ ] TestWatchDirectory - detects dir changes
- [ ] TestWatcherStop - stops watching

---

## Implementation Notes
- Updater tests may need to mock HTTP (acceptable exception)
- Watcher tests use real temp files
- Tutorial tests use tea.Msg simulation","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:22:01.856984-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T15:57:28.037454-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T15:57:28.037454-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-5b7","depends_on_id":"ntm-idx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:22:34.445501-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-hzl","title":"Define persona configuration schema","description":"# Define Persona Configuration Schema

## Purpose

Create the TOML schema for defining agent personas.

## Schema Definition

```toml
# ~/.config/ntm/personas.toml (or in config.toml)

[[personas]]
name = "architect"
description = "Senior software architect focused on system design"
agent_type = "claude"      # cc, cod, gmi
model = "opus"             # Model alias or full name
system_prompt = """
You are a senior software architect...
"""
temperature = 0.7          # Optional, agent-specific
context_files = [          # Optional, files to include in context
    "ARCHITECTURE.md",
    "docs/design/*.md"
]
tags = ["design", "review"]  # For filtering/discovery

[[personas]]
name = "implementer"
description = "Fast implementation engineer"
agent_type = "claude"
model = "sonnet"
system_prompt = """
You are an implementation engineer...
"""
temperature = 0.3
```

## Go Types

```go
type Persona struct {
    Name          string   `toml:"name"`
    Description   string   `toml:"description"`
    AgentType     string   `toml:"agent_type"`
    Model         string   `toml:"model"`
    SystemPrompt  string   `toml:"system_prompt"`
    Temperature   *float64 `toml:"temperature,omitempty"`
    ContextFiles  []string `toml:"context_files,omitempty"`
    Tags          []string `toml:"tags,omitempty"`
}

type PersonasConfig struct {
    Personas []Persona `toml:"personas"`
}
```

## Loading Strategy

1. Load built-in personas (compiled in)
2. Load user personas from ~/.config/ntm/personas.toml
3. Load project personas from .ntm/personas.toml
4. Later definitions override earlier (project > user > builtin)

## Built-in Personas

Include 5 default personas:
- architect
- implementer
- reviewer
- tester
- documenter

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] TOML schema defined and documented
- [ ] Go types with proper validation
- [ ] Multi-source loading with override semantics
- [ ] Built-in personas compiled in
- [ ] ntm config init includes persona examples","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:23:45.54837-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T04:32:58.545336-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T04:32:58.545336-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-i0zz","title":"Research agent CLI capabilities for prompt/model injection","description":"# Research Agent CLI Capabilities

## Purpose

Before implementing Personas and Multi-Model, we need to understand what each agent CLI actually supports.

## Questions to Answer

### Claude Code
- [ ] How to specify model? (--model flag?)
- [ ] How to inject system prompt? (--system-prompt, file-based?)
- [ ] Does it support temperature control?
- [ ] What environment variables affect behavior?

### Codex CLI  
- [ ] How to specify model? (-m flag)
- [ ] System prompt support?
- [ ] Temperature or other parameters?
- [ ] Environment variables?

### Gemini CLI
- [ ] How to specify model? (--model flag?)
- [ ] System prompt support? (--system-instruction?)
- [ ] Other parameters?

## Research Method

1. Check CLI --help output
2. Read documentation
3. Test with actual commands
4. Document findings

## Output

Create reference document:
```markdown
# Agent CLI Reference

## Claude Code
- Model: `--model <name>` or CLAUDE_MODEL env var
- System prompt: `--system-prompt <text>` or `.claude/system-prompt.md`
- Temperature: Not supported
- Other: ...

## Codex
...

## Gemini
...
```

## Why This Matters

- Personas need to inject system prompts - only works if CLI supports it
- Multi-Model needs to specify models - need correct flag
- Can't implement features for capabilities that don't exist

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Claude Code capabilities documented
- [ ] Codex capabilities documented
- [ ] Gemini capabilities documented
- [ ] Limitations clearly identified
- [ ] Reference document created","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:41:11.699998-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T04:28:18.770992-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T04:28:18.770992-05:00"}

-{"id":"ntm-i5x","title":"Implement --robot-graph for cross-tool correlation map","description":"Map relationships between agents, beads, and mail threads.

## Rationale
In a complex project, an orchestrator needs to understand HOW things are connected:
- Which agent is working on which bead?
- What mail threads relate to which work?
- What's the dependency graph of in-progress work?

This correlation enables intelligent orchestration decisions.

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "generated_at": "ISO8601",
  "assignments": [
    {
      "agent": "myproject:0.1",
      "agent_type": "claude",
      "beads": ["ntm-1rz"],
      "mail_threads": ["TKT-45", "TKT-52"]
    }
  ],
  "bead_graph": {
    "ntm-1rz": {
      "status": "in_progress",
      "assigned_to": "0.1",
      "blocked_by": [],
      "blocking": ["ntm-abc", "ntm-def"]
    },
    "ntm-abc": {
      "status": "open",
      "assigned_to": null,
      "blocked_by": ["ntm-1rz"],
      "blocking": []
    }
  },
  "mail_summary": {
    "TKT-45": {
      "subject": "Profiler implementation",
      "participants": ["BlueLake", "GreenCastle"],
      "last_activity": "ISO8601",
      "unread": 2
    }
  },
  "orphan_beads": ["ntm-xyz"],  // beads with no agent assigned
  "orphan_threads": []           // threads not linked to any bead
}
```

## Data Sources
- Agent assignments: parse bead comments/updates for agent mentions
- Mail threads: agent-mail project queries
- Bead graph: bd dep show

## Use Cases
1. Visualize project state at a glance
2. Find orphaned work (unassigned beads)
3. Trace communication around specific tasks
4.  {"id":"ntm-idx","title":"SUB-EPIC: Unit Test Coverage","description":"# SUB-EPIC: Unit Test Coverage

## Scope
Unit tests for ALL packages in NTM, targeting 80%+ coverage on non-TUI and 60%+ on TUI packages.

## Philosophy: No Mocks Where Possible
- **tmux package**: Use real tmux sessions (create test sessions, destroy after)
- **config package**: Use real temp files and directories
- **output package**: Use real io.Writers
- **status package**: Use real output strings
- **watcher package**: Use real file events

## Coverage Targets by Package

### Critical Priority (0% currently)
1. internal/cli - All 20+ commands need basic execution tests
2. internal/robot - JSON output verification
3. internal/palette - Model/Update/View cycle tests
4. internal/tui/* - Component rendering tests

### High Priority (low coverage)
5. internal/tmux - Session/Pane operations (5.4% → 80%)
6. internal/config - Config loading/parsing (16.2% → 80%)

### Medium Priority (decent coverage)
7. internal/output - Formatting functions (55.1% → 85%)
8. internal/status - Pattern detection (69.6% → 85%)

### Lower Priority (but still needed)
9. internal/tutorial - Slide navigation
10. internal/updater - Version comparison
11. internal/watcher - Debouncer behavior

## Test Structure
Each package should have:
- `*_test.go` file co-located with source
- Table-driven tests where applicable
- Helper functions for test setup/teardown
- Skip conditions for environment-specific tests

## Blocking
This epic blocks the Integration and E2E test epics.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:19.451033-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:19.451033-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-idx","depends_on_id":"ntm-l5h","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:05.855961-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"ntm-ign","title":"Implement --robot-tail for multi-pane output streaming","description":"Tail recent output from all panes in a session, structured for AI consumption.

## Rationale
Knowing agents EXIST isn't enough - I need to see what they're DOING. Current approach requires separate tmux capture-pane calls per pane, each returning raw terminal output. This consolidates into one structured query.

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-tail myproject --lines=20
ntm --robot-tail myproject --panes=0.1,0.2 --lines=50
```

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "captured_at": "ISO8601",
  "panes": {
    "0.1": {
      "type": "claude",
      "state": "active",
      "lines": ["line1", "line2", ...],
      "truncated": false
    },
    "0.2": {
      "type": "codex",  
      "state": "idle",
      "lines": [...],
      "truncated": true
    }
  }
}
```

## Design Decisions
- Lines are raw strings (preserve formatting for context)
- Include agent type/state metadata inline (no second query needed)
- truncated=true means more output exists beyond --lines limit
- Empty panes return empty lines array (not omitted)
- Filter out ANSI codes but preserve newlines

## Use Cases
1. Check if agent is making progress
2. See error messages without attaching
3. Validate agent understood instructions
4. Detect stuck/looping agents","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:50:44.336167-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:17:07.432795-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:17:07.432795-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-uwa","title":"Implement Agent Mail server detection and config","description":"# Implement Agent Mail Server Detection and Config

## Purpose

Before using any Agent Mail features, NTM needs to detect if the server is running and load configuration.

## Configuration Schema

Add to ~/.config/ntm/config.toml:
```toml
[agent_mail]
enabled = true                              # Master toggle
url = "http://127.0.0.1:8765/mcp/"          # Server endpoint
token = ""                                  # Bearer token (or use env var)
auto_register = true                        # Auto-register sessions as agents
program_name = "ntm"                        # Program identifier for registration
```

Environment variable overrides:
- AGENT_MAIL_URL
- AGENT_MAIL_TOKEN
- AGENT_MAIL_ENABLED (0/1)

## Detection Logic

```go
func (c *Client) IsAvailable() bool {
    // 1. Check if enabled in config
    // 2. Attempt health_check() with 2s timeout
    // 3. Cache result for 30s to avoid repeated checks
}

func (c *Client) RequireAvailable() error {
    if !c.IsAvailable() {
        return ErrAgentMailUnavailable
    }
    return nil
}
```

## Integration Points

1. **ntm deps**: Show Agent Mail status
   ```
   Agent Mail: ✓ running (http://127.0.0.1:8765/mcp/)
   Agent Mail: ✗ not detected (optional)
   ```

2. **Config loading**: internal/config loads agent_mail section
3. **Lazy initialization**: Client created on first use, not at startup
4. **Status command**: Show connection status in ntm status

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Config schema added with sensible defaults
- [ ] Environment variables override config
- [ ] Detection is fast (<2s timeout) and cached
- [ ] ntm deps shows Agent Mail status
- [ ] Graceful behavior when server unavailable","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"LilacLake","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:01.67162-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:32:32.28481-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T03:32:32.28481-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-uwa","depends_on_id":"ntm-ceb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:55.651713-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

-{"id":"ntm-vr2","title":"SUB-EPIC: E2E Tests with Detailed Logging","description":"# SUB-EPIC: End-to-End (E2E) Tests with Detailed Logging

## Scope
Complete workflow tests that exercise NTM from CLI invocation to tmux state verification.

## E2E Test Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Full Agent Workflow
1. ntm spawn project --cc=2 --cod=1
2. Verify session created with 4 panes (3 agents + 1 user)
3. ntm send project --cc 'hello world'
4. Verify prompt delivered to Claude panes only
5. ntm interrupt project
6. Verify Ctrl+C sent
7. ntm kill -f project
8. Verify session destroyed

### Scenario 2: Command Palette Workflow
1. Create session with agents
2. Invoke palette (programmatic, not TUI)
3. Select command
4. Verify prompt sent to targets

### Scenario 3: Quick Setup Workflow
1. ntm quick testproject --template=go
2. Verify directory structure created
3. Verify git initialized
4. Verify .vscode and .claude dirs created

### Scenario 4: Session Persistence
1. Create session, add agents
2. Detach
3. Re-attach
4. Verify state preserved

### Scenario 5: Robot Mode Output
1. Create various session states
2. Run --robot-status, --robot-plan
3. Verify JSON output matches actual state
4. Parse and validate JSON schema

## Logging Requirements

### Per-Test Log File
```
test_logs/
├── TestFullAgentWorkflow_20251210_103045.log
├── TestCommandPaletteWorkflow_20251210_103047.log
└── ...
```

### Log Content Structure
```
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] TEST START: TestFullAgentWorkflow
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] SETUP: Creating test session 'ntm_test_abc123'
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] EXEC: ntm spawn ntm_test_abc123 --cc=2 --cod=1
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] STDOUT: Session created: ntm_test_abc123 (4 panes)
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] VERIFY: Checking pane count...
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] ASSERT PASS: pane_count == 4
[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z] EXEC: ntm send ntm_test_abc123 --cc 'hello'
...
[2025-12-10T10:30:48Z] TEARDOWN: Killing session ntm_test_abc123
[2025-12-10T10:30:48Z] TEST END: PASS (3.2s)
```

### System State Capture
Before and after each test:
- tmux list-sessions
- tmux list-panes (for test session)
- Environment variables
- Working directory

## Implementation
- tests/e2e/ directory for all E2E tests
- Shared test harness with logging
- CI-compatible (skip if tmux unavailable)
- Parallel execution with unique session names","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:52.553331-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:52.553331-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-vr2","depends_on_id":"ntm-l5h","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:06.044553-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"ntm-vr2","depends_on_id":"ntm-7lk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:06.232148-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-vum","title":"EPIC: MCP Agent Mail Integration","description":"# MCP Agent Mail Integration

## Background & Motivation

NTM orchestrates multiple AI coding agents (Claude, Codex, Gemini) in parallel tmux panes. Currently, these agents operate in isolation - they can see the same filesystem but cannot:
- Communicate with each other directly
- Know what others are working on
- Coordinate file access to avoid conflicts
- Share status or hand off work

MCP Agent Mail provides exactly this coordination layer.

## CRITICAL CLARIFICATION: NTM's Role

**NTM is a COORDINATOR and BRIDGE, not an agent replacement.**

The agents running in panes (Claude Code, Codex, etc.) may or may not have their own Agent Mail integration:
- If they DO have Agent Mail support: NTM provides unified visibility (aggregate inbox, lock status)
- If they DON'T: NTM acts as coordinator, sending messages on behalf of the user

**Key insight**: NTM doesn't pretend to BE the agents. It's the orchestration layer that:
1. Provides a unified view of all agent mail activity
2. Allows the USER to send messages to/between agents
3. Shows file reservation status across all agents
4. Detects conflicts before spawning new agents

## What NTM's Agent Mail Integration Provides

### 1. Visibility Layer
- Aggregate inbox view: See messages across all registered agents
- File lock status: Who has reserved what files
- Agent directory: Which agents are active in the project

### 2. User-to-Agent Communication
```bash
ntm mail send myproject --to cc_1 "Please focus on the API endpoint"
```
This sends FROM the NTM coordinator TO the specified agent. It's the user speaking through NTM.

### 3. Session Coordination
```bash
ntm lock myproject "src/api/**" --reason "User-directed refactor"
```
NTM can reserve files on behalf of the session, signaling to all agents that these files are being coordinated.

### 4. Conflict Prevention
```bash
ntm spawn myproject --cc=2
# Warning: src/api/** is reserved by GreenCastle (cc_1 in session 'other')
# Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Technical Approach

MCP Agent Mail runs as an HTTP server (default port 8765) with JSON-RPC 2.0.

NTM will:
1. **Create internal/agentmail package**: HTTP client for Agent Mail MCP tools
2. **Register as coordinator**: `ntm_<session>` identity for user-initiated actions
3. **Aggregate views**: Fetch inbox/locks from all known agents
4. **Expose commands**: ntm mail, ntm lock, ntm locks
5. **Dashboard integration**: Show mail and lock status
6. **Graceful degradation**: All features optional if Agent Mail unavailable

## Key Design Decisions

- **NTM is a coordinator**: Sends messages as `ntm_<session>`, not as individual agents
- **Agents own their mail**: If Claude Code has Agent Mail support, it handles its own inbox
- **NTM aggregates and visualizes**: Unified view across all project agents
- **Optional integration**: Works without Agent Mail; features degrade gracefully
- **No duplication**: Don't re-implement what agents already do

## What This Is NOT

- NOT a replacement for agent-side Agent Mail integration
- NOT pretending NTM "is" the Claude/Codex/Gemini agent
- NOT required for basic NTM functionality

## Success Criteria

- [ ] NTM can detect if Agent Mail server is running
- [ ] NTM registers coordinator identity per session
- [ ] Aggregate inbox shows messages across all project agents
- [ ] File reservation status visible in status/dashboard
- [ ] Conflicts detected before spawning in reserved areas
- [ ] Graceful degradation when Agent Mail unavailable","status":"in_progress","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","assignee":"BrownMountain","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:17:05.78207-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T10:59:51.052088-05:00"}
-{"id":"ntm-vz9","title":"Implement ntm copy command with filtering","description":"Copy pane content to system clipboard with filtering options.

## What This Task Does

Create 'ntm copy' command to copy pane output to system clipboard.

## Interface

# Copy entire pane output
$ ntm copy myproject:1

# Copy last N lines
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --last 50

# Copy lines matching pattern
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --pattern 'ERROR.*'

# Copy only code blocks (markdown fences)
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --code

# Copy to file instead of clipboard
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --output result.txt

# Quiet mode (no confirmation message)
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --quiet

## Content Filtering

--last N: Capture last N lines of pane output
--pattern: Filter to lines matching regex
--code: Extract markdown code blocks only

Code block extraction:
```python
def hello():
    print('world')
```

Becomes:
def hello():
    print('world')

Multiple code blocks concatenated with newlines.

## Implementation

1. Capture pane content (existing CapturePaneOutput)
2. Apply filters in order:
   a. --last (if specified)
   b. --pattern (if specified)
   c. --code (if specified)
3. Copy to clipboard or file
4. Show confirmation unless --quiet

## JSON Output

$ ntm copy myproject:1 --json
{
  "source": "myproject:1",
  "lines": 42,
  "bytes": 1234,
  "destination": "clipboard"
}

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Basic copy to clipboard works
- [ ] --last N limits lines
- [ ] --pattern filters by regex
- [ ] --code extracts code blocks
- [ ] --output writes to file
- [ ] --quiet suppresses output
- [ ] --json shows copy metadata

func createTempConfig(t *testing.T, content string) string {
    f, _ := os.CreateTemp("", "ntm-config-*.toml")
    f.WriteString(content)
    f.Close()
    t.Cleanup(func() { os.Remove(f.Name()) })
    return f.Name()
}

 {"id":"ntm-ws2","title":"Create internal/output package with JSON/text formatters","description":"Create a unified output formatting system that all commands will use.

## What This Task Does

Create a new internal/output package that provides:
1. OutputFormat type (JSON, Text, Table)
2. Formatter interface that commands implement
3. Root-level --json flag handling
4. Consistent JSON schema patterns

## Why This Is First

This is the foundation for all JSON output. Every command will use this
package, so it needs to be designed well upfront.

## Technical Details

Package structure:
internal/output/
├── format.go      # OutputFormat enum, Formatter interface
├── json.go        # JSON-specific formatting helpers
├── text.go        # Text-specific formatting (existing patterns)
└── schema.go      # Common JSON schema types

Key types:
type OutputFormat int
const (
    FormatText OutputFormat = iota
    FormatJSON
)

type Formatter interface {
    Format(data interface{}, format OutputFormat, w io.Writer) error
}

Common JSON patterns:
- Timestamps as ISO 8601 strings
- Errors include 'error' field with message
- Lists are always arrays (even empty)
- Nested objects for complex data

## Implementation Steps

1. Create internal/output directory
2. Define OutputFormat enum and detection logic
3. Create Formatter interface
4. Add JSON helper functions (marshal with indent, error wrapping)
5. Add root --json flag to cobra root command
6. Create context passing for format flag

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] internal/output package exists with clean API
- [ ] --json flag added to root command
- [ ] Format detection works (flag, env var, pipe detection)
- [ ] JSON output is consistently formatted
- [ ] Package is documented with examples

```go
func createTestSession(t *testing.T) string {
    name := fmt.Sprintf("ntm_test_%d", time.Now().UnixNano())
    t.Cleanup(func() { KillSession(name) })
    CreateSession(name, os.TempDir())
    return name
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-xkvu","title":"Implement file modification tracking per agent","description":"# Implement File Modification Tracking Per Agent

## Purpose

Track which files each agent modifies, enabling conflict detection and contribution attribution.

## Approach

### Method 1: File Mtime Tracking
```go
type FileSnapshot struct {
    Path  string
    Mtime time.Time
    Size  int64
}

func SnapshotDirectory(dir string) map[string]FileSnapshot {
    // Walk directory, record mtime/size for each file
}

func DetectChanges(before, after map[string]FileSnapshot) []FileChange {
    // Compare snapshots, return changed files
}
```

### Method 2: Git Status Parsing
```go
func GetUncommittedChanges() ([]string, error) {
    // git status --porcelain
    // Parse output for modified/added/deleted files
}
```

### Attribution to Agents
Track changes with timestamps:
```go
type AgentChange struct {
    Agent     string    // Pane identifier
    File      string
    Timestamp time.Time
    ChangeType string  // modified, added, deleted
}
```

When `ntm send` completes, snapshot files again and attribute changes to the targeted agents.

## Limitations

- Can't perfectly attribute if multiple agents running simultaneously
- Best-effort: attribute to agent(s) that were sent prompts most recently
- Works best with sequential workflows

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Snapshot file state before prompts
- [ ] Detect changes after prompts complete
- [ ] Attribute changes to agents (best-effort)
- [ ] Store change history for comparison

 {"id":"ntm-xle","title":"Implement cross-platform clipboard detection and access","description":"Create internal/clipboard package for cross-platform clipboard access.

## What This Task Does

Create a clipboard abstraction that works on macOS, Linux (X11/Wayland),
and WSL. This provides the foundation for clipboard-related features.

## Platform Detection

Detect platform and available clipboard tools:

macOS:
- pbcopy / pbpaste (always available)

Linux X11:
- xclip -selection clipboard
- xsel --clipboard

Linux Wayland:
- wl-copy / wl-paste

WSL:
- clip.exe (write only)
- powershell.exe Get-Clipboard (read)

Fallback:
- tmux's internal clipboard (with warning about limitations)

## Implementation

package clipboard

type Clipboard interface {
    Copy(content string) error
    Paste() (string, error)
    Available() bool
}

func New() (Clipboard, error) {
    switch runtime.GOOS {
    case "darwin":
        return &macOSClipboard{}, nil
    case "linux":
        if isWSL() {
            return &wslClipboard{}, nil
        }
        if isWayland() {
            return &waylandClipboard{}, nil
        }
        return &x11Clipboard{}, nil
    }
    return &tmuxClipboard{}, nil
}

## Error Handling

- Tool not found: Clear error with installation suggestion
- Permission denied: Suggest running with appropriate access
- Empty clipboard: Return empty string, not error

## Testing

Need to handle:
- CI environments (no display, no clipboard)
- SSH sessions (no local clipboard)
- Container environments

Skip clipboard tests or mock in these environments.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Clipboard works on macOS
- [ ] Clipboard works on Linux X11
- [ ] Clipboard works on Linux Wayland
- [ ] Clipboard works on WSL
- [ ] Graceful fallback when tools missing
- [ ] Clear error messages

Enables unified state queries, delta updates, batch operations, and cross-tool correlation. Priority based on orchestration value.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:49:44.994993-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T02:49:44.994993-05:00","comments":[{"id":9,"issue_id":"ntm-y44","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Orchestrator's Perspective: Why These Flags Matter

This epic represents a fundamental shift in how NTM serves AI orchestrators. The existing --robot-status flag is just the beginning. Here's the complete vision:

### Core Philosophy
An AI orchestrator managing 10+ agents across multiple sessions needs:
1. **Complete situational awareness** in a single query
2. **Token efficiency** to maximize useful work vs overhead
3. **Fast feedback loops** for responsive orchestration
4. **Cross-tool correlation** (tmux ↔ beads ↔ agent-mail)

### The Token Economics
Consider orchestrating 8 agents:
- Current approach: 8+ tool calls, ~2000 tokens of output to parse
- With --robot-snapshot: 1 call, ~500 tokens, everything I need
- With --since delta: 1 call, ~50 tokens for incremental updates

This is a 40x improvement in polling scenarios.

### Dependency Structure
The implementation follows a logical build-up:
1. **Foundation**: Agent detection (ntm-3n9) + Alert system (ntm-rlp)
2. **Integrations**: Agent-mail (ntm-gk5) + Beads (ntm-l5y)
3. **Core**: Unified snapshot (ntm-sim)
4. **Extensions**: Delta mode (ntm-dvz), Terse (ntm-oba), Health (ntm-c36)
5. **Advanced**: Graph (ntm-i5x), Assign (ntm-20n), Interrupt (ntm-pb3)

### Success Criteria
- Single --robot-snapshot call provides complete state
- Delta queries return only changes (95% token savings)
- All output is structured JSON, no parsing human text
- Cross-tool correlation works (know which agent → which bead → which thread)
- Batch operations support multi-agent commands","created_at":"2025-12-10T07:55:21Z"},{"id":13,"issue_id":"ntm-y44","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Revised Architecture: Minimal Dependencies

After review, the dependency structure has been revised. Core robot flags should work IMMEDIATELY without requiring integrations. Integrations ENHANCE but don't BLOCK.

### Tier 1: Core (No blockers - implement first)
- **ntm-sim**: --robot-snapshot - unified state query
- **ntm-ign**: --robot-tail - multi-pane output
- **ntm-d2t**: --robot-send - batch messaging  
- **ntm-c36**: --robot-health - health matrix
- **ntm-oln**: --robot-spawn - structured session creation
- **ntm-pb3**: --robot-interrupt - priority course correction

### Tier 2: Extensions (depend on Tier 1)
- **ntm-dvz**: --robot-snapshot --since - delta updates (needs ntm-sim + ntm-hat)
- **ntm-oba**: --robot-terse - compact output (needs ntm-sim)
- **ntm-a8q**: --robot-ack - send confirmation

### Tier 3: Enhancements (enhance Tier 1 but don't block)
- **ntm-3n9**: Agent detection (improves accuracy of all queries)
- **ntm-rlp**: Alert generation (adds alerts to snapshot/health)
- **ntm-gk5**: Agent-mail integration (adds mail data to snapshot)
- **ntm-l5y**: Beads integration (adds bead data to snapshot)
- **ntm-hat**: State tracking ring buffer (enables delta mode)

### Tier 4: Advanced Features
- **ntm-20n**: --robot-assign - work distribution recommendations
- **ntm-i5x**: --robot-graph - cross-tool correlation
- **ntm-sc6**: Progress detection
- **ntm-fpb**: Command history tracking
- **ntm-lla**: Session save/restore

### Implementation Order Recommendation
1. Core Tier 1 flags (can be implemented in parallel)
2. State tracking (ntm-hat) for delta support
3. Delta mode (ntm-dvz) 
4. Enhancements as needed
5. Advanced features based on user demand","created_at":"2025-12-10T07:59:37Z"},{"id":14,"issue_id":"ntm-y44","author":"jemanuel","text":"## Child Tasks

This epic tracks the following tasks:

### Extensions (Tier 2)

### Enhancements (Tier 3)

- ntm-gk5: Agent-mail integration

### Advanced Features (Tier 4)

- ntm-i5x: --robot-graph (correlation map)

- ntm-fpb: Command history tracking
- ntm-lla: Session save/restore

Total: 19 tasks across 4 tiers","created_at":"2025-12-10T08:00:30Z"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-y7l","title":"Implement agent tagging in pane titles","description":"Store and parse agent tags in tmux pane titles.

## What This Task Does

Extend the pane title format to include user-defined tags:
- Current: session__cc_1
- New: session__cc_1[frontend,refactor]

## Tag Storage Format

Tags are comma-separated within square brackets at the end of pane title.

Examples:
- myproject__cc_1[frontend]
- myproject__cod_1[backend,api,urgent]
- myproject__gmi_1[] (no tags, explicit empty)

## Implementation

Add to internal/tmux package:

func SetPaneTags(session string, paneIndex int, tags []string) error {
    currentTitle := GetPaneTitle(session, paneIndex)
    baseTitle := stripTags(currentTitle)
    newTitle := baseTitle + "[" + strings.Join(tags, ",") + "]"
    return exec.Command("tmux", "select-pane", "-t", target, "-T", newTitle).Run()
}

func GetPaneTags(session string, paneIndex int) []string {
    title := GetPaneTitle(session, paneIndex)
    // Parse [tag1,tag2] from end of title
    ...
}

## Integration with spawn/add

$ ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --tags=frontend
# Creates: myproject__cc_1[frontend], myproject__cc_2[frontend]

$ ntm add myproject --cod=1 --tags=backend,api
# Creates: myproject__cod_1[backend,api]

## Tag modification

$ ntm tag myproject:1 --add=urgent
$ ntm tag myproject:1 --remove=frontend
$ ntm tag myproject:1 --set=backend,api

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Tags stored in pane title
- [ ] Tags parsed correctly from title
- [ ] --tags flag works on spawn command
- [ ] --tags flag works on add command
- [ ] ntm tag command for modification
- [ ] Tags survive session detach/reattach
